examples.title: Basic LCG Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of Linear Congruential Generator with configurable parameters
examples.explanation: Standard LCG implementation showing the recurrence relation. Park-Miller uses prime modulus 2³¹-1 and multiplier 16807 (primitive root) to achieve period m-1.

examples.title: Hull-Dobell Verification
examples.description: Code to verify if LCG parameters satisfy Hull-Dobell conditions for full period
examples.explanation: Implements Hull-Dobell Theorem conditions to check if LCG can achieve maximum period. Returns boolean and reason for failure if not achievable.

examples.title: LCG Parameter Recovery Attack
examples.description: Demonstrates how to recover LCG parameters from consecutive outputs (educational purpose)
examples.code:
def recover_lcg_params(x0, x1, x2):
 """
 Recover parameters (a, c, m) from 3 consecutive outputs.
 Uses: x1 = (a*x0 + c) mod m
 x2 = (a*x1 + c) mod m
 Subtracting: x2 - x1 = a*(x1 - x0) mod m
 
 This is a simplified version. Full attack uses lattice reduction.
 """
 # Solve for a: a = (x2 - x1) / (x1 - x0) mod m
 # Without knowing m, this is under-determined
 
 # For demonstration: find m as GCD of differences
 d1 = x1 - x0
 d2 = x2 - x1
 m_candidate = abs(d2 - d1) # Simplified estimate
 
 # In practice: use lattice reduction for large parameters
 print(f"Given: X₀={x0}, X₁={x1}, X₂={x2}")
 print(f"Differences: d1={d1}, d2={d2}")
 print("Full attack requires lattice reduction (LLL algorithm)")

# Example with known parameters
# m=31, a=3, c=0, seed=1
# Sequence: 1, 3, 9, 27, 19, ...
recover_lcg_params(1, 3, 9)
examples.explanation: Shows the basic idea behind LCG parameter recovery. Real attacks use Coppersmith's algorithm or LLL lattice reduction. This explains why LCGs are cryptographically insecure.
examples.timeComplexity: O(k³) for lattice reduction where k is parameter dimension
